content: It’s natural to question what really causes the seasons, especially since explanations can sometimes seem overcomplicated. Here’s the straightforward truth: Earth orbits the Sun once a year, but it’s not just about how far we are from the Sun. Instead, Earth is tilted slightly on its axis—about 23.5 degrees—and this tilt stays pointed in the same direction as we move around the Sun. Because of this, different parts of Earth get more direct sunlight at different times of the year. When your part of the world leans toward the Sun, sunlight hits more directly and days are longer, making it warmer—this is summer. When it leans away, sunlight is weaker and days are shorter, causing winter. So, the seasons come from the angle and intensity of sunlight changing throughout the year, not from changes in distance to the Sun. You can even see this in how the Sun’s height in the sky shifts day by day.
